claimed_contribution: Provides an editorial framework for JMR centered on boundary-spanning research and specifies four paper types the journal will especially encourage.
Clarifies theory-driven empirical work by distinguishing structural modeling, theory-derived empirical tests that are not fully structural, and theory-driven behavioral experimentation.
Emphasizes the value of richer and combined data sources, including greater use of field and natural experiments in marketing research.
context: This article is an editorial statement about publication priorities for the Journal of Marketing Research in academic marketing. It discusses how marketing scholarship can better connect behavioral and quantitative traditions and increase relevance to managers, consumers, policy officials, and nonspecialists.
design_and_data: This is a conceptual editorial essay rather than an empirical study. The author proposes and explains four categories of submissions that can "span the boundaries" of marketing research. It includes methodological discussion (e.g., structural models, lab and field experiments, natural experiments) but reports no original dataset, sample, or statistical estimation.
main_findings: The paper identifies four priority submission categories for JMR: out-of-the-box, interdisciplinary, theory-driven empirical, and papers using novel or combined data sets.
Theory-driven empirical research is presented as work that links data analysis to explicit theory, either through structural models or through theory-derived empirical implications.
The four categories are not mutually exclusive, and JMR remains open to high-quality work beyond these categories.
one_line_summary: This editorial sets JMR's boundary-spanning agenda by prioritizing out-of-the-box, interdisciplinary, theory-driven empirical, and novel-data research submissions.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces an editorial agenda that builds on existing methodological conversations (including structural modeling) and pushes marketing research toward more interdisciplinary, theory-grounded, and data-rich work.
research_gap: The article argues that marketing research can be limited by existing boundaries across disciplines, methods, and audiences, and by empirical work not sufficiently anchored in theory. It also notes that some high-potential approaches, especially natural experiments, have relatively few applications in marketing.
research_question: What kinds of papers should JMR prioritize to increase the impact and relevance of marketing research as the field and practice evolve? The paper asks how boundary-spanning work across theory, methods, data sources, and audiences can better guide future submissions.
